Notebook name

Cape & Cart BI Dashboard

Exported at 11:13 PM GMT+2 on June 18, 2026

Cape & Cart H1 Growth — BI Dashboard Co-authored with CoCo

6 key Business Intelligence (BI) sections from CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH (ANALYTICS + GOLD layers)

Currency standard: all revenue, AOV, basket, product, category, and margin values are shown in South African Rand (ZAR / R).

Why Rand matters: Cape & Cart sells to South African customers, pays local media and fulfilment costs in Rand, and judges campaign payback against local gross margin. Keeping the dashboard in ZAR avoids exchange-rate noise and lets the reader compare revenue, spend, AOV, margin, and ROAS on the same commercial basis.

Abbreviation guide: this dashboard uses a few analytics and retail terms. They are expanded here so the reader can follow the case study without guessing.

  • BI: Business Intelligence, the reporting layer that turns data into charts, KPIs, and decisions.
  • H1: first half of the year, covering January to June 2024 in this case study.
  • ZAR / R: South African Rand, the currency used for revenue, spend, basket value, and margin.
  • AOV: Average Order Value, calculated as revenue divided by orders.
  • ROAS: Return on Ad Spend, calculated as attributed revenue divided by advertising spend.
  • KPI: Key Performance Indicator, a headline metric used to judge business performance.
  • VIP: Very Important / high-value customer segment, usually customers with higher spend or stronger repeat purchase behaviour.
  • ML: Machine Learning, models that use historical data to forecast, score, or recommend next actions.
  • GA4: Google Analytics 4, the web and app analytics source for customer behaviour signals.
1. Growth trend
Shows whether first-half (H1) momentum came from order volume, basket value, or both.
2. Channel mix
Compares revenue, order count, and Average Order Value (AOV) by acquisition channel.
3. Category economics
Separates high-revenue categories from high-margin categories.
4. Customer base
Shows the mix of new, returning, lapsed, and VIP customers.
5. Campaign attribution
Ranks products that deserve more budget against products that need fixing.
6. Executive scorecard
Summarises H1 revenue in Rand, orders, customers, and product coverage.
7. Behaviour correlations
Tests which customer behaviours move revenue and repeat purchase.
8. Spend efficiency
Shows whether more media spend is creating profitable attributed revenue.
9. Actions
Turns the analysis into budget, product, and retention decisions.
PythonSetup[42]
from snowflake.snowpark.context import get_active_session
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

session = get_active_session()
session.sql("USE WAREHOUSE CAPE_CART_WH").collect()
plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 100

1. Revenue (ZAR / Rand) & Orders — Monthly Trend

What this chart explains: the dual-axis view compares monthly revenue in Rand against order volume, so the reader can see whether growth came from more customers checking out or from larger baskets.

  • Why it matters: revenue alone can hide weak demand if AOV is doing all the work; orders alone can hide discount-driven growth.
  • How to read it: rising bars with rising line means healthy demand; rising revenue with flat orders points to basket-size or mix gains.
  • Action: use the strongest month as the campaign benchmark and investigate months where revenue and orders move in different directions.
SQLMonthly Revenue (ZAR / Rand) & Orders[43]
SELECT LEFT(ORDER_DATE, 7) AS MONTH,
       COUNT(*) AS ORDERS,
       SUM(ORDER_TOTAL_ZAR::FLOAT) AS REVENUE_ZAR,
       AVG(ORDER_TOTAL_ZAR::FLOAT) AS AOV_ZAR
FROM CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.ANALYTICS.ORDERS
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
6 rows
MONTH
ORDERS
REVENUE_ZAR
AOV_ZAR

410.91445158103
Python1. Dual-Axis Revenue (ZAR / Rand) & Orders Chart[44]
df = monthly_trends.to_pandas()

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

ax1.bar(df['MONTH'], df['REVENUE_ZAR'], color='steelblue', alpha=0.7, label='Revenue (ZAR / Rand)')
ax1.set_xlabel('Month')
ax1.set_ylabel('Revenue (ZAR / Rand)', color='steelblue')
ax1.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='steelblue')
ax1.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'R{x/1e6:.2f}M'))

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(df['MONTH'], df['ORDERS'], color='coral', marker='o', linewidth=2.5, label='Orders')
ax2.set_ylabel('Orders', color='coral')
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='coral')

fig.suptitle('Monthly Revenue (ZAR / Rand) & Order Volume — H1 2024', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
fig.legend(loc='upper left', bbox_to_anchor=(0.1, 0.88))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"H1 Total Revenue: R{df['REVENUE_ZAR'].sum():,.2f}")
print(f"H1 Total Orders: {df['ORDERS'].sum():,}")
print(f"H1 Avg AOV: R{df['AOV_ZAR'].mean():.2f}")
All monetary values are South African Rand (ZAR / R).
H1 Total Revenue: R5,038,057.79
H1 Total Orders: 12,100
H1 Avg AOV: R416.37

2. Channel Performance

What this chart explains: the three-bar comparison shows each channel's revenue, order contribution, and Average Order Value (AOV) side by side.

  • Why it matters: a channel can look strong on revenue but weak on Average Order Value (AOV), also called basket value, or efficient on orders but too small to scale.
  • How to read it: channels that rank well across revenue and AOV are quality growth engines; high-order, low-AOV channels need merchandising or bundling support.
  • Action: protect budget for channels that bring both volume and value, then test creative or audience changes on weaker channels.
SQLChannel Performance Data[45]
SELECT * FROM CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.ANALYTICS.VW_CHANNEL_PERFORMANCE

Python2. Triple Bar Chart — Channel Performance[46]
df = channel_perf.to_pandas()
df['REVENUE_ZAR'] = pd.to_numeric(df['REVENUE_ZAR'], errors='coerce')
df['ORDERS'] = pd.to_numeric(df['ORDERS'], errors='coerce')
df['AOV_ZAR'] = pd.to_numeric(df['AOV_ZAR'], errors='coerce')
df = df.sort_values('REVENUE_ZAR', ascending=True)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))
fig.suptitle('Channel Performance — Revenue (ZAR / Rand), Orders & AOV', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

colors = plt.cm.Set2(np.linspace(0, 1, len(df)))

axes[0].barh(df['ATTRIBUTION_CHANNEL'], df['REVENUE_ZAR'], color=colors)
axes[0].set_title('Revenue (ZAR / Rand)')
axes[0].xaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'R{x/1e3:.0f}K'))
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='x')

axes[1].barh(df['ATTRIBUTION_CHANNEL'], df['ORDERS'], color=colors)
axes[1].set_title('Orders')
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='x')

axes[2].barh(df['ATTRIBUTION_CHANNEL'], df['AOV_ZAR'], color=colors)
axes[2].set_title('AOV (ZAR / Rand)')
axes[2].set_xlim(350, 500)
axes[2].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='x')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3. Product Categories

What this chart explains: the category view separates net revenue in Rand from margin rate, showing which parts of the catalogue are large and which are profitable.

  • Why it matters: the campaign should not only chase sales; it should scale categories that can absorb media cost and still protect gross margin.
  • How to read it: large bubbles or bars with strong margin are scale candidates; high revenue with thin margin needs pricing, shipping, or discount review.
  • Action: build bundles around high-margin categories and reduce discount pressure where revenue is high but margin is fragile.
SQLCategory Performance[47]
SELECT CATEGORY,
       SUM(NET_REVENUE_ZAR) AS NET_REVENUE_ZAR,
       SUM(UNITS_SOLD) AS UNITS_SOLD,
       AVG(GROSS_MARGIN_PCT) AS AVG_MARGIN
FROM CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.GOLD.GOLD_PRODUCT_PERFORMANCE
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
11 rows

Python3. Revenue Bars + Bubble Scatter[56]
df = category_perf.to_pandas()
df['NET_REVENUE_ZAR'] = pd.to_numeric(df['NET_REVENUE_ZAR'], errors='coerce')
df['UNITS_SOLD'] = pd.to_numeric(df['UNITS_SOLD'], errors='coerce')
df['AVG_MARGIN'] = pd.to_numeric(df['AVG_MARGIN'], errors='coerce')

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
fig.suptitle('Product Categories — Net Revenue (ZAR / Rand) & Margin', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

colors = plt.cm.RdYlGn(df['AVG_MARGIN'] / df['AVG_MARGIN'].max())
axes[0].barh(df['CATEGORY'], df['NET_REVENUE_ZAR'], color=colors)
axes[0].set_title('Revenue by Category (color = margin %)')
axes[0].xaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'R{x/1e3:.0f}K'))
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='x')

sc = axes[1].scatter(df['UNITS_SOLD'], df['NET_REVENUE_ZAR'], s=df['AVG_MARGIN']*800,
               c=df['AVG_MARGIN'], cmap='RdYlGn', alpha=0.8, edgecolors='black')
for _, row in df.iterrows():
    axes[1].annotate(row['CATEGORY'], (row['UNITS_SOLD'], row['NET_REVENUE_ZAR']),
                    fontsize=7, ha='left', va='bottom')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Units Sold')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Net Revenue (ZAR / Rand)')
axes[1].set_title('Volume vs Revenue (bubble = margin)')
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.colorbar(sc, ax=axes[1], label='Margin %')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4. Customer Segments

What this chart explains: the segment view shows how H1 customers split across new, returning, lapsed, and Very Important / high-value customer (VIP) groups.

  • Why it matters: acquisition growth is expensive if new buyers do not return; retention and VIP share decide whether the campaign creates long-term value.
  • How to read it: a large new-customer share is good only if returning and VIP segments are also growing behind it.
  • Action: trigger post-purchase email, product recommendations, and loyalty offers within the first 30 days after a first order.
SQLCustomer Segments[49]
SELECT CUSTOMER_SEGMENT, COUNT(*) AS CUSTOMERS
FROM CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.ANALYTICS.CUSTOMERS
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
4 rows
CUSTOMER_SEGMENT
CUSTOMERS
1
New
6395
2
Returning
2817
3
Lapsed Reactivated
702
4
Vip
436
Python4. Pie + Bar — Customer Segments[50]
df = segments.to_pandas()
df['CUSTOMERS'] = pd.to_numeric(df['CUSTOMERS'], errors='coerce')
total = df['CUSTOMERS'].sum()
df['PCT'] = df['CUSTOMERS'] / total * 100

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))
fig.suptitle('Customer Segments — Distribution', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

colors = ['#4e79a7', '#f28e2b', '#e15759', '#76b7b2']

axes[0].pie(df['CUSTOMERS'], labels=[f"{s}\n({p:.0f}%)" for s, p in zip(df['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT'], df['PCT'])],
           colors=colors, startangle=90, textprops={'fontsize': 11})
axes[0].set_title('Share of Customers')

bars = axes[1].bar(df['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT'], df['CUSTOMERS'], color=colors, edgecolor='black', alpha=0.85)
axes[1].set_ylabel('Customer Count')
axes[1].set_title('Segment Counts')
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='y')
for bar, pct in zip(bars, df['PCT']):
    axes[1].text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2., bar.get_height() + 50,
                f'{int(bar.get_height()):,}\n({pct:.0f}%)', ha='center', fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5. Campaign Attribution — Top 10 vs Worst 10

What this chart explains: the product Return on Ad Spend (ROAS) ranking shows the products where campaign spend is earning back strongly and the products where spend is under-performing.

  • Why it matters: product-level attribution turns a generic media report into a trading plan: scale winners, diagnose losers.
  • How to read it: the top 10 are candidates for more budget, more stock, and stronger landing pages; the worst 10 need creative, price, audience, or margin review.
  • Action: move budget toward the top products only after checking stock and margin; pause, fix, or retest the bottom products before spending more.
SQLProduct Campaign Attribution[51]
SELECT * FROM CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.ANALYTICS.PRODUCT_CAMPAIGN_ATTRIBUTION_SUMMARY

Python5. ROAS Top vs Worst — Channel Colored[52]
df = attribution.to_pandas()
df['ROAS'] = pd.to_numeric(df['ROAS'], errors='coerce')
df['ATTRIBUTED_REVENUE_ZAR'] = pd.to_numeric(df['ATTRIBUTED_REVENUE_ZAR'], errors='coerce')
df['ATTRIBUTED_SPEND_ZAR'] = pd.to_numeric(df['ATTRIBUTED_SPEND_ZAR'], errors='coerce')

top = df[df['RANK_GROUP'] == 'top_10'].sort_values('ROAS', ascending=True)
worst = df[df['RANK_GROUP'] == 'worst_10'].sort_values('ROAS', ascending=True)

channel_colors = {'Google Ads': '#4285F4', 'CM360': '#34A853', 'DV360': '#FBBC05',
                  'YouTube': '#EA4335', 'Email': '#9C27B0'}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 7))
fig.suptitle('Campaign Attribution — Product ROAS (Top 10 vs Worst 10)', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

bars = axes[0].barh(top['PRODUCT_NAME'], top['ROAS'],
                   color=[channel_colors.get(c, 'gray') for c in top['LEAD_CHANNEL']],
                   edgecolor='black', alpha=0.85)
axes[0].set_title('TOP 10 — Scale Budget', fontsize=12, color='green')
axes[0].set_xlabel('ROAS')
axes[0].axvline(x=3.0, color='green', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='x')
for bar, ch in zip(bars, top['LEAD_CHANNEL']):
    axes[0].text(bar.get_width() + 0.05, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
                f'{bar.get_width():.1f}x ({ch})', va='center', fontsize=8)

bars2 = axes[1].barh(worst['PRODUCT_NAME'], worst['ROAS'],
                    color=[channel_colors.get(c, 'gray') for c in worst['LEAD_CHANNEL']],
                    edgecolor='black', alpha=0.85)
axes[1].set_title('WORST 10 — Pause/Fix/Retest', fontsize=12, color='red')
axes[1].set_xlabel('ROAS')
axes[1].axvline(x=1.0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis='x')
for bar, ch in zip(bars2, worst['LEAD_CHANNEL']):
    axes[1].text(bar.get_width() + 0.02, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
                f'{bar.get_width():.1f}x ({ch})', va='center', fontsize=8)

from matplotlib.patches import Patch
legend_elements = [Patch(facecolor=c, label=l, edgecolor='black') for l, c in channel_colors.items()]
fig.legend(handles=legend_elements, loc='lower center', ncol=5, fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.03, 1, 0.95])
plt.show()

6. Executive Summary

What this chart explains: the KPI card row gives executives the H1 answer in one glance: revenue in Rand, orders, customers, and product coverage.

  • Why it matters: leaders need the scale of the campaign before they inspect the detail.
  • How to read it: treat these cards as the control totals that all downstream channel, product, and ML stories must reconcile to.
  • Action: use this scorecard as the opening slide for the case study and as the monthly governance baseline.
SQLExecutive Scorecard[53]
SELECT * FROM CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.ANALYTICS.VW_CAMPAIGN_EXECUTIVE_SCORECARD
1 row
CASE_STUDY
GA4_EVENTS
GOOGLE_ADS_ROWS
YOUTUBE_ROWS
ORDERS_H1
REVENUE_ZAR
CUSTOMERS
PRODUCTS
1
Cape & Cart H1 Growth Campaign
5150
2080
1545
12100
5038057.79
10350
30
Python6. Executive KPI Cards[54]
df = exec_scorecard.to_pandas()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 3.5))
ax.axis('off')

metrics = {
    'Orders H1': f"{int(df['ORDERS_H1'].iloc[0]):,}",
    'Revenue H1': f"R{float(df['REVENUE_ZAR'].iloc[0]):,.0f}",
    'Customers': f"{int(df['CUSTOMERS'].iloc[0]):,}",
    'Products': f"{int(df['PRODUCTS'].iloc[0])}",
}

for i, (label, value) in enumerate(metrics.items()):
    x = 0.125 + i * 0.22
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x - 0.08, 0.15), 0.16, 0.7, transform=ax.transAxes,
                               facecolor='#f0f4f8', edgecolor='#d0d8e0', linewidth=1.5, zorder=0))
    ax.text(x, 0.65, value, fontsize=22, fontweight='bold', ha='center', va='center',
           transform=ax.transAxes, color='#1a365d')
    ax.text(x, 0.3, label, fontsize=11, ha='center', va='center', color='#4a5568',
           transform=ax.transAxes)

fig.suptitle('H1 2024 — Executive Summary KPIs', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', y=0.98)
fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

7. Scatter Plots & Correlations — Customer Behavior

What this chart explains: the scatter and correlation views test how customer behaviour metrics relate to revenue, frequency, basket value, and engagement.

  • Why it matters: this is the bridge from Business Intelligence (BI) to Machine Learning (ML): a model needs predictive signals, not only totals.
  • How to read it: tight upward patterns suggest useful predictors; scattered clouds warn that transactional data alone may not be enough.
  • Action: enrich the feature store with Google Analytics 4 (GA4) behaviour such as product views, add-to-cart events, checkout starts, discount usage, and email clicks.
SQLCustomer-Level Metrics[57]
SELECT c.CUSTOMER_ID,
       c.CUSTOMER_SEGMENT,
       COUNT(o.ORDER_ID) AS ORDER_COUNT,
       SUM(o.ORDER_TOTAL_ZAR::FLOAT) AS TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR,
       AVG(o.ORDER_TOTAL_ZAR::FLOAT) AS AVG_ORDER_VALUE,
       MAX(o.ORDER_DATE) AS LAST_ORDER_DATE,
       DATEDIFF('day', MIN(o.ORDER_DATE)::DATE, MAX(o.ORDER_DATE)::DATE) AS CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS
FROM CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.ANALYTICS.CUSTOMERS c
JOIN CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.ANALYTICS.ORDERS o ON c.CUSTOMER_ID = o.CUSTOMER_ID
GROUP BY 1, 2

Python7. Scatter Plots & Correlation Matrix[58]
from scipy import stats

df = customer_metrics.to_pandas()
for c in ['ORDER_COUNT','TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR','AVG_ORDER_VALUE','CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS']:
    df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 11))
fig.suptitle('Customer Behavior — Scatter Plots & Correlations', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

seg_colors = {'New': '#4e79a7', 'Returning': '#f28e2b', 'Lapsed Reactivated': '#e15759', 'Vip': '#76b7b2'}

for seg in df['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT'].unique():
    sub = df[df['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT'] == seg]
    axes[0][0].scatter(sub['ORDER_COUNT'], sub['TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR'], alpha=0.4, s=20,
                      color=seg_colors.get(seg, 'gray'), label=seg)
slope, intercept, r, p, se = stats.linregress(df['ORDER_COUNT'].dropna(), df['TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR'].dropna())
x_line = np.linspace(df['ORDER_COUNT'].min(), df['ORDER_COUNT'].max(), 100)
axes[0][0].plot(x_line, slope * x_line + intercept, 'k--', linewidth=2, label=f'R²={r**2:.3f}')
axes[0][0].set_xlabel('Order Count')
axes[0][0].set_ylabel('Total Revenue (ZAR / Rand)')
axes[0][0].set_title('Orders vs Revenue (Linear Regression)')
axes[0][0].legend(fontsize=7)
axes[0][0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

for seg in df['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT'].unique():
    sub = df[df['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT'] == seg]
    axes[0][1].scatter(sub['CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS'], sub['AVG_ORDER_VALUE'], alpha=0.4, s=20,
                      color=seg_colors.get(seg, 'gray'), label=seg)
axes[0][1].set_xlabel('Customer Tenure (days)')
axes[0][1].set_ylabel('Avg Order Value (ZAR)')
axes[0][1].set_title('Tenure vs AOV by Segment')
axes[0][1].legend(fontsize=7)
axes[0][1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

numeric_cols = ['ORDER_COUNT', 'TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR', 'AVG_ORDER_VALUE', 'CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS']
corr_matrix = df[numeric_cols].corr()
im = axes[1][0].imshow(corr_matrix, cmap='RdBu_r', vmin=-1, vmax=1)
axes[1][0].set_xticks(range(len(numeric_cols)))
axes[1][0].set_yticks(range(len(numeric_cols)))
axes[1][0].set_xticklabels(['Orders', 'Revenue', 'AOV', 'Tenure'], fontsize=8, rotation=45)
axes[1][0].set_yticklabels(['Orders', 'Revenue', 'AOV', 'Tenure'], fontsize=8)
for i in range(len(numeric_cols)):
    for j in range(len(numeric_cols)):
        axes[1][0].text(j, i, f'{corr_matrix.iloc[i, j]:.2f}', ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9)
axes[1][0].set_title('Correlation Matrix')
plt.colorbar(im, ax=axes[1][0])

seg_stats = df.groupby('CUSTOMER_SEGMENT').agg(
    avg_orders=('ORDER_COUNT', 'mean'),
    avg_revenue=('TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR', 'mean'),
    avg_aov=('AVG_ORDER_VALUE', 'mean'),
    count=('CUSTOMER_ID', 'count')
).reset_index()
axes[1][1].scatter(seg_stats['avg_orders'], seg_stats['avg_revenue'],
                  s=seg_stats['count']/5, alpha=0.8,
                  c=[seg_colors.get(s, 'gray') for s in seg_stats['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT']],
                  edgecolors='black')
for _, row in seg_stats.iterrows():
    axes[1][1].annotate(f"{row['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT']}\n(n={int(row['count']):,})",
                       (row['avg_orders'], row['avg_revenue']),
                       fontsize=9, ha='center', va='bottom')
axes[1][1].set_xlabel('Avg Orders per Customer')
axes[1][1].set_ylabel('Avg Revenue per Customer (ZAR)')
axes[1][1].set_title('Segment Positioning (bubble = count)')
axes[1][1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

8. Regression Analysis — Spend vs Revenue (Campaign ROAS)

What this chart explains: the regression and efficiency view checks whether campaign spend is translating into attributed revenue and Return on Ad Spend (ROAS).

  • Why it matters: spend can rise without creating profitable growth; this view separates scale from efficiency.
  • How to read it: points above the trend or with higher ROAS are better candidates for budget scaling; points below trend need diagnosis before extra spend.
  • Action: set budget guardrails by channel and product so scaling decisions follow expected Rand return, not just impressions or clicks.
SQLCampaign Spend vs Revenue[59]
SELECT PRODUCT_NAME, CATEGORY, LEAD_CHANNEL,
       ATTRIBUTED_SPEND_ZAR::FLOAT AS SPEND,
       ATTRIBUTED_REVENUE_ZAR::FLOAT AS REVENUE,
       ROAS::FLOAT AS ROAS,
       ATTRIBUTED_CONVERSIONS::INT AS CONVERSIONS,
       RANK_GROUP, RECOMMENDED_ACTION
FROM CAPE_CART_H1_GROWTH.ANALYTICS.PRODUCT_CAMPAIGN_ATTRIBUTION_SUMMARY

Python8. Regression — Spend vs Revenue & Efficiency[60]
from scipy.stats import linregress

df = campaign_regression.to_pandas()
for c in ['SPEND','REVENUE','ROAS','CONVERSIONS']:
    df[c] = pd.to_numeric(df[c], errors='coerce')

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 11))
fig.suptitle('Regression & Efficiency Analysis — Campaign Attribution', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')

channel_colors = {'Google Ads': '#4285F4', 'CM360': '#34A853', 'DV360': '#FBBC05',
                  'YouTube': '#EA4335', 'Email': '#9C27B0'}

for ch in df['LEAD_CHANNEL'].unique():
    sub = df[df['LEAD_CHANNEL'] == ch]
    axes[0][0].scatter(sub['SPEND'], sub['REVENUE'], s=60, alpha=0.8,
                      color=channel_colors.get(ch, 'gray'), label=ch, edgecolors='black', linewidth=0.5)

slope, intercept, r, p, se = linregress(df['SPEND'], df['REVENUE'])
x_reg = np.linspace(df['SPEND'].min(), df['SPEND'].max(), 100)
axes[0][0].plot(x_reg, slope * x_reg + intercept, 'k--', linewidth=2,
               label=f'y={slope:.1f}x + {intercept:.0f}\nR²={r**2:.3f}')
axes[0][0].set_xlabel('Attributed Spend (ZAR)')
axes[0][0].set_ylabel('Attributed Revenue (ZAR / Rand)')
axes[0][0].set_title('Spend vs Revenue — Linear Regression')
axes[0][0].legend(fontsize=7)
axes[0][0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

for ch in df['LEAD_CHANNEL'].unique():
    sub = df[df['LEAD_CHANNEL'] == ch]
    axes[0][1].scatter(sub['SPEND'], sub['ROAS'], s=60, alpha=0.8,
                      color=channel_colors.get(ch, 'gray'), label=ch, edgecolors='black', linewidth=0.5)
axes[0][1].axhline(y=3.0, color='green', linestyle='--', alpha=0.6, label='Target ROAS=3x')
axes[0][1].axhline(y=1.0, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.6, label='Breakeven=1x')
axes[0][1].set_xlabel('Spend (ZAR)')
axes[0][1].set_ylabel('ROAS')
axes[0][1].set_title('Spend vs ROAS — Efficiency Frontier')
axes[0][1].legend(fontsize=7)
axes[0][1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

for ch in df['LEAD_CHANNEL'].unique():
    sub = df[df['LEAD_CHANNEL'] == ch]
    axes[1][0].scatter(sub['CONVERSIONS'], sub['REVENUE'], s=sub['ROAS']*20, alpha=0.7,
                      color=channel_colors.get(ch, 'gray'), edgecolors='black', linewidth=0.5)
slope2, intercept2, r2, _, _ = linregress(df['CONVERSIONS'], df['REVENUE'])
x2 = np.linspace(df['CONVERSIONS'].min(), df['CONVERSIONS'].max(), 100)
axes[1][0].plot(x2, slope2 * x2 + intercept2, 'k--', linewidth=2, label=f'R²={r2**2:.3f}')
axes[1][0].set_xlabel('Conversions')
axes[1][0].set_ylabel('Revenue (ZAR / Rand)')
axes[1][0].set_title('Conversions vs Revenue (bubble = ROAS)')
axes[1][0].legend(fontsize=8)
axes[1][0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

channel_agg = df.groupby('LEAD_CHANNEL').agg(
    total_spend=('SPEND', 'sum'),
    total_revenue=('REVENUE', 'sum'),
    avg_roas=('ROAS', 'mean'),
    total_conversions=('CONVERSIONS', 'sum')
).reset_index()
channel_agg['efficiency'] = channel_agg['total_revenue'] / channel_agg['total_spend']

axes[1][1].scatter(channel_agg['total_spend'], channel_agg['total_revenue'],
                  s=channel_agg['total_conversions']/2, alpha=0.8,
                  c=[channel_colors.get(ch, 'gray') for ch in channel_agg['LEAD_CHANNEL']],
                  edgecolors='black', linewidth=1.5)
for _, row in channel_agg.iterrows():
    axes[1][1].annotate(f"{row['LEAD_CHANNEL']}\nROAS={row['efficiency']:.1f}x",
                       (row['total_spend'], row['total_revenue']),
                       fontsize=9, ha='center', va='bottom', fontweight='bold')
axes[1][1].set_xlabel('Total Spend (ZAR)')
axes[1][1].set_ylabel('Total Revenue (ZAR / Rand)')
axes[1][1].set_title('Channel Efficiency (bubble = conversions)')
axes[1][1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

9. Actionable Insights & Recommendations

What this section explains: the final BI layer converts the charts into decisions for budget allocation, merchandising, retention, and data quality.

  • Why it matters: a dashboard only creates value when it changes the next action.
  • How to read it: recommendations should trace back to the chart evidence above: trend, channel, product, customer, and spend efficiency.
  • Action: make the recommendations the operating backlog for the next campaign sprint.
Python9. Actionable Insights Summary[61]
cust = customer_metrics.to_pandas()
for c in ['ORDER_COUNT','TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR','AVG_ORDER_VALUE','CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS']:
    cust[c] = pd.to_numeric(cust[c], errors='coerce')

camp = campaign_regression.to_pandas()
for c in ['SPEND','REVENUE','ROAS','CONVERSIONS']:
    camp[c] = pd.to_numeric(camp[c], errors='coerce')

slope_cust, _, r_cust, _, _ = stats.linregress(cust['ORDER_COUNT'].dropna(), cust['TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR'].dropna())
slope_camp, _, r_camp, _, _ = linregress(camp['SPEND'], camp['REVENUE'])

vip = cust[cust['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT'] == 'Vip']
new = cust[cust['CUSTOMER_SEGMENT'] == 'New']

top_channel = camp.groupby('LEAD_CHANNEL')['ROAS'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
worst_channel = camp.groupby('LEAD_CHANNEL')['ROAS'].mean().sort_values(ascending=True)

high_spend_low_roas = camp[(camp['SPEND'] > camp['SPEND'].median()) & (camp['ROAS'] < 2.0)]

print('=' * 70)
print('         ACTIONABLE INSIGHTS & RECOMMENDATIONS')
print('=' * 70)

print(f'''
--- REGRESSION INSIGHTS ---

1. CUSTOMER ORDER-REVENUE REGRESSION (R² = {r_cust**2:.3f})
   Each additional order = +R{slope_cust:.0f} revenue per customer.
   ACTION: Loyalty programs targeting 2nd/3rd purchase drive
   predictable revenue growth.

2. CAMPAIGN SPEND-REVENUE REGRESSION (R² = {r_camp**2:.3f})
   Each R1 spend generates R{slope_camp:.1f} in attributed revenue.
   ACTION: Spend is productive — scale budget on high-ROAS channels.
''')

print(f'''--- SEGMENT INSIGHTS ---

3. VIP CUSTOMERS (n={len(vip):,})
   Avg Revenue: R{vip["TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR"].mean():,.0f} | Avg Orders: {vip["ORDER_COUNT"].mean():.1f}
   ACTION: VIPs are {vip["TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR"].mean()/new["TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR"].mean():.1f}x more valuable
   than New customers. Invest in retention & upsell programs.

4. NEW CUSTOMERS (n={len(new):,}, 62% of base)
   Avg Revenue: R{new["TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR"].mean():,.0f} | Avg Orders: {new["ORDER_COUNT"].mean():.1f}
   ACTION: Convert 10% of New → Returning = +{int(len(new)*0.1)} high-value
   customers. Focus on post-purchase nurture sequences.
''')

print(f'''--- CHANNEL EFFICIENCY ---

5. BEST CHANNEL: {top_channel.index[0]} (Avg ROAS = {top_channel.iloc[0]:.1f}x)
   ACTION: Scale budget +15-20%. High confidence in ROI.

6. WORST CHANNEL: {worst_channel.index[0]} (Avg ROAS = {worst_channel.iloc[0]:.1f}x)
   ACTION: Pause underperforming creatives, A/B test new audiences,
   or reallocate budget to top performers.
''')

if len(high_spend_low_roas) > 0:
    waste = high_spend_low_roas['SPEND'].sum()
    print(f'''7. BUDGET WASTE ALERT: {len(high_spend_low_roas)} products with high spend & ROAS < 2x
   Total at-risk spend: R{waste:,.0f}
   Products: {", ".join(high_spend_low_roas["PRODUCT_NAME"].tolist())}
   ACTION: Immediately pause or restructure these campaigns.
''')

print(f'''--- CORRELATION INSIGHTS ---

8. ORDER COUNT ↔ REVENUE: r = {cust["ORDER_COUNT"].corr(cust["TOTAL_REVENUE_ZAR"]):.3f}
   Strong positive — frequency drives value.
   ACTION: Implement frequency-based loyalty tiers.

9. TENURE ↔ AOV: r = {cust["CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS"].corr(cust["AVG_ORDER_VALUE"]):.3f}
   {'Positive' if cust['CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS'].corr(cust['AVG_ORDER_VALUE']) > 0 else 'Weak/Negative'}
   — longer customers {'spend more per order' if cust['CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS'].corr(cust['AVG_ORDER_VALUE']) > 0.1 else 'dont necessarily spend more per order'}.
   ACTION: {'Upsell premium products to loyal customers.' if cust['CUSTOMER_TENURE_DAYS'].corr(cust['AVG_ORDER_VALUE']) > 0.1 else 'Focus on increasing order frequency rather than basket size for retained customers.'}
''')
print('=' * 70)
======================================================================
         ACTIONABLE INSIGHTS & RECOMMENDATIONS
======================================================================

--- REGRESSION INSIGHTS ---

1. CUSTOMER ORDER-REVENUE REGRESSION (R² = 0.502)
   Each additional order = +R419 revenue per customer.
   ACTION: Loyalty programs targeting 2nd/3rd purchase drive
   predictable revenue growth.

2. CAMPAIGN SPEND-REVENUE REGRESSION (R² = 0.808)
   Each R1 spend generates R5.5 in attributed revenue.
   ACTION: Spend is productive — scale budget on high-ROAS channels.

--- SEGMENT INSIGHTS ---

3. VIP CUSTOMERS (n=305)
   Avg Revenue: R719 | Avg Orders: 1.7
   ACTION: VIPs are 1.0x more valuable
   than New customers. Invest in retention & upsell programs.

4. NEW CUSTOMERS (n=4,424, 62% of base)
   Avg Revenue: R710 | Avg Orders: 1.7
   ACTION: Convert 10% of New → Returning = +442 high-value
   customers. Focus on post-purchase nurture sequences.

--- CHANNEL EFFICIENCY ---

5. BEST CHANNEL: Google Ads (Avg ROAS = 4.3x)
   ACTION: Scale budget +15-20%. High confidence in ROI.

6. WORST CHANNEL: DV360 (Avg ROAS = 1.5x)
   ACTION: Pause underperforming creatives, A/B test new audiences,
   or reallocate budget to top performers.

7. BUDGET WASTE ALERT: 3 products with high spend & ROAS < 2x
   Total at-risk spend: R47,400
   Products: Outdoor Beanbag, Luxury Throw Blanket, Round Pet Bed
   ACTION: Immediately pause or restructure these campaigns.

--- CORRELATION INSIGHTS ---

8. ORDER COUNT ↔ REVENUE: r = 0.709
   Strong positive — frequency drives value.
   ACTION: Implement frequency-based loyalty tiers.

9. TENURE ↔ AOV: r = 0.018
   Positive
   — longer customers dont necessarily spend more per order.
   ACTION: Focus on increasing order frequency rather than basket size for retained customers.

======================================================================
